Source: scratch.md

TODO

  • Revise output collect (pip_collect_out)
    • deprecate group parameter and group column in pipeline object; instead provide same (and richer) functionality via pip_collect_out() using tags
  • Update README
  • Update vignette 7 (pipeflow vs targets package)
    • replace system.time by individual microbenchmark calls?
  • Update vignette 1 (getting started)

I’d like to polish both the README and vignette 7 (pipeflow vs targets package) before publishing to CRAN. Let’s first revise the introduction text in the README up until the bullet points (which we do afterwards). For v0.2.3, while not explicitly referring the targets package, I did on purpose emphazise points that stand in contrast to the targets package

targets key features:

Function-based paradigm — workflows are defined as lists of target objects using tar_target() External _targets.R script file required — pipeline is defined in a separate file In-memory object passing — targets pass objects through memory (with file-based storage in _targets/) Heavy package dependencies (targets, tarchetypes, crew, etc.) Complex build system — tar_make() runs in a new R process File tracking — tracks files for changes via file targets Data store — stores output in _targets/ directory Static and dynamic branching — comprehensive branching capabilities High-performance computing — supports distributed computing via crew Cloud storage — supports cloud storage formats Comprehensive metadata — tracks code hashes, error info, timing Debugging tools — tar_traceback(), tar_load(), etc. Visual network graphs — tar_visnetwork() Cues — fine-grained invalidation rules Pipeline as code — pipeline defined declaratively in target script Steep learning curve — multiple files, conventions, functions to learn pipeflow key features:

Script/object-based paradigm — steps added dynamically with pip_add() Interactive/incremental building — no separate script file needed; pipeline built in R session All-in-one — pipeline, execution, and state management in one object Lightweight — minimal dependencies (data.table) In-memory — results stored directly in the pipeline object Step modification — insert, remove, rename, replace steps at any time Parameter tracking — pip_get_params() / pip_set_params() auto-propagate changes Split/map/reduce — built-in execution modes for partitioned data Self-modifying/recursive pipelines — steps can return modified pipelines Views — filtered sub-pipelines by tags, groups, or step indices Step locking — prevent accidental modifications Graph visualization — pip_get_graph() compatible with visNetwork Low learning curve — R-native, few concepts Pipeline states — tracks new/done/outdated/failed/error states Auto invalidation — downstream steps auto-marked outdated Key contrasts for the README:

Paradigm: pipeflow is interactive/incremental (build-while-coding), targets is declarative (define-then-run) Learning curve: pipeflow has deliberately low learning curve, targets has steeper learning curve Dependencies: pipeflow is lightweight (data.table), targets has many dependencies Flexibility: pipeflow allows modifying pipelines at any stage (insert/remove/replace), targets pipeline is more static once defined Branching: targets has comprehensive static/dynamic branching; pipeflow has simpler split/map/reduce Storage: pipeflow stores everything in-memory in R objects; targets stores in file store Scale: targets designed for heavy workloads with distributed computing; pipeflow lighter-weight Modularity: pipeflow has views/tags/groups for organizing; targets has target grouping Self-modification: pipeflow supports recursive self-modifying pipelines; targets is more static Now let me make concrete suggestions for the README. I’ll focus on:

Adding a “Comparison with targets” section A comparison table similar to makeit’s approach Key differentiators that position pipeflow Let me formulate the suggestions

├── _targets.R ├── data.csv ├── R/ │ ├── functions.R

  • easier to debug
  • discuss/challenge the coding/pipeline design philosopy wrt functions distributed across files are more manageable than one big pipeline block - rather to the contrary … plus we have modern code editors …
  • less dependencies
  • dependency verified when it’s created (or removed)
  • can we move lgr, jsonlite and even R6 dependencies already to “Suggested”?

gh pr create –base main –head fast_dag –title “feat: fast C++ DAG engine and pip_* functional API” –body @’ ## Summary

This PR replaces the original R-based dependency resolution with a high-performance and for pipelines optimized C++ implementation of a DAG and introduces a modern pip_* functional API.

Core Changes

  • C++ DAG engine (src/dag.cpp, 882 lines): All graph operations (node/edge add/remove, topological ordering, reachability queries) now run at C++ speed via Rcpp external pointers and .Call interface. This eliminates the R object overhead for dependency resolution.
  • New and improved pip_* API: Functional API replacing the R6 Pipeline class — pip_new(), pip_add(), pip_run(), pip_replace(), pip_clone(), pip_bind(), etc.
  • Execution modes (auto/split/reduce/plain): Native support for map-reduce style workflows where steps can split output into named partitions and downstream steps auto-map over them.
  • Backward compatibility: Legacy pipe_* functions and Pipeline R6 class preserved as deprecated aliases (R/aliases.R, R/pipelineR6.R).

New Features

  • pip_view() — create filtered pipeline views (by dependencies, tags)
  • pip_tag() / pip_untag()
  • [[ extract operator for pipelines
  • Comprehensive revision of all vignettes to match new API

Other

  • MIT license, updated CI workflows, dependabot config, kilo.json, lintr/styler config
  • Extensive test suite (~10K+ lines) covering C++ DAG, pip_* API, aliases, execution modes, and recursive runs

On this page

Developed by Roman Pahl.
